text: The 12mm port is going to go in the right upper quadrant and then there's two 8mm ports that go in the left upper quadrant.
Occasionally we will put in a 5mm assist port to aid in suctioning.
So we generally harvest in a mental flap when we do these cases and so the greater momentum has to be taken down off of the transverse colon.
And this can be somewhat tricky.
I find it a little easier to do this laparoscopically, to be honest, than using the robot, but with perseverance you get there.
And so we're going to try to find a pedicle, a vein, and an artery off of the gastroepiploic artery that we're going to use as our supply to the flap.
The vessel sealing device that you see there is very helpful in dividing this stuff.
So I have a tip-up grasper retracting in my furthest right-hand port, and then my other right-hand port is a bipolar dissector, and then a fenestrated bipolar in the left hand.
So there's the piece of momentum that we're going to use for our mental flap, and that's the vascular pedicle you can see there.
So we have some additional work to get that taken down off of the colon, which you see here.
You don't want to harvest too much Amentum because it can be quite bulky, especially in more adipose patients.
And that can sometimes cause atelectasis when you bring it up into the chest.
So you just want enough to be able to wrap around the anastomosis really.

So there's our mental flap there, and what we're going to do now is once that's harvested, we have that flipped over the stomach in a cephalad fashion, and we're going to continue to divide the omentum towards the pylorus.
This is the area where you have to be really careful about the pedicle of the gastroepiploic artery, the right gastroepiploic.
You can start seeing it right in there.
This fellow had a little bit of fatty tissue but sometimes you can actually see the vein.
So I like to do this dissection with the bipolar cautery.
And then once that's done, then we continue up along the greater curvature of the stomach.
So here we're dividing the short gastric vessels.
And the vessel sealer seems very reliable for this.
I've never had a problem with bleeding from an incompletely sealed vessel.
And here's sometimes where an assistant can be helpful to put a 5mm grasper in and retract down on the top of the pancreas and the colon there.
Now we're looking over the top of the cardi of the stomach and we're just taking down some additional attachments to the mentum and spleen.
So the stomach is being retracted down towards the patient's umbilicus in that view.
And then coming up to the left cruce.
So we never divide the cruce, either of them.
You really don't need to.
Some last short gastric attachments.
And we're not going to do too much dissection up into the hiatus at this point because we don't want to run the risk of getting a pneumothorax and compromising on pneumoperitoneum.
But we'll do this dissection a lot more thoroughly later on.
So now the stomach is lifted anteriorly, and this exposes the left gastric vein and the artery behind it.
So we'll usually dissect out that left gastric vein and then get around it and divide it with a vessel sealing device.
Or you can use hemlock clips.
The vein, it's fine to use the vessel sealing device.
The artery, probably not.
So now at this point we're going to dissect the left gastric artery.
And sometimes this is better from the right side of the stomach, so we'll open up the lesser mentum here now and get into the lesser sac.
Now this particular patient, he ended up having a fairly large replaced left hepatic artery, and so you'll see how we managed to deal with that.
The robot actually is quite nice for dissecting that out and preserving it.
There it is there is coming into view
So the key that I found is to just skeletonize that thing and then find the branches or branch to the stomach and then divide that.
Sometimes I guess when these are small you can just buzz right through them but this one looked a little large so I wanted to keep it
So there's the branch there that you can see to the stomach.
And sometimes there's more than one, so you've got to be careful of that.
And this we're going to divide with a little hemlock clip, at least on the proximal side.
Now the vessel sealer is going to be used to cut the artery.
And that looks like a vessel, but I think it's just vagal branches.
So now we're going to take all that tissue, cephalad, to the common artery and take that up with the specimen.
And there we're identifying the right cruce now at this point.
And all of that tissue will be taken down to the level of the descending aorta.
Celiac axis lymph nodes are taken out.
So to do this, I just divide the fat until I come down onto the common hepatic artery and then, using careful blunt dissection, make some planes and then divide them.
I'm going to be a little bit careful here because the portal vein can be a little more cephalad sometimes, so you obviously don't want to encounter that.
So a good amount of spreading first before you cut is a good idea.
There's the left gastric there going down to the portal.
So we'll take this all the way down to the IVC.
Then take it out as a separate specimen.
So that little suction catheter is just a tracheal suction catheter that we hook up to a 5mm sucker and put through a 5mm port.
It's quite nice because you get to control the suction yourself.
You can't apply the suction, but you can control where the suction tip is.
So now we divide the tissue at the level of the crow's foot.
Then once that's done, then we'll create our conduit with the robotic staplers.
I generally prefer to use a green load.
I think you probably could use a blue and thinner stomachs.
Try to make a fairly narrow conduit, about five centimeters or so.
You get some estimation of the size of it from the tip-up grasper.
Very important to make sure that the anesthesiologist has withdrawn the nasogastric tube at this point.
So we want to try to put some downward retraction to sort of elongate the stomach a little bit.
It's also important to just be cognizant that you don't want to have any kind of twisting of the conduit when you fire your staple line.
It typically takes about five or six firings.
We're going to completely divide the conduit from the proximal stomach and then suture the tip of the conduit to the stomach remnant to pull it up into the chest.
So now we're going to assess the perfusion of the conduit.
And we do this by using indesignine green, which comes in a 25 milligram bottle.
This is just very reassuring.
I think ideally it would be nice to be able to have some kind of objective measurement of perfusion, but for right now we just sort of eyeball it.
But you can see in this particular case that really the entire conduit is perfused, except perhaps the top little bit right there.
But that part's going to come out anyhow in our
once we do our anastomosis in the chest.
So I generally try to put a little stitch where I think the watershed area is just to remind me to try and put the anastomosis below that.
And you can see that our mental flap is also nicely perfused
So there's our stitch going in and we want to put our anastomosis below that area.
And then finally, once that's done, then we do a hiatal dissection.
So normally we would also put in a laparoscopic or a feeding jejunostomy tube, which is very difficult I think with the robot just because of the port placement that you have.
And so typically at the end of the case I'll revert to laparoscopy and just pop a J-tube in.
But, in this particular case, he had a J-tube already put in because he had some dysphagia to begin with.
So he had a J-tube already in place, which sped things up a little bit for us.
So just getting right down onto the aorta and taking all of that tissue.
We really want to try and get as much radial margin as we possibly can.
And it's not infrequent to get into the left pleur when you do this.
Again, staying as far away from the wall of the esophagus as possible.
You want to get right on the pericardium there.
Again, we never divide the cruci or the hiatus.
It's not necessary.
So there's the pericardium there.
And you can really dissect quite far.
I think you can probably get all the way up to the carina if you persisted.
Things to watch out for here, obviously, are the inferior pulmonary veins.
So we'll usually mobilize a good, I don't know, six or seven centimeters or so up into the hiatus because it really makes your dissection from the chest a whole lot easier when you've done this.
And in this particular patient, I believe we got into the left pleura, so I'll often just put a chest tube in from one of the abdominal ports, and usually a Blake drain is all you need.
So there's the drain going in.
And we just take that through one of the 8mm ports.
Important, obviously, to get all of the drain into the chest so it doesn't drain peritoneal fluid.

And then the tip of the conduit, as well as the tip of the mental flap that we harvested, both of those are sewn then to the remnant of the stomach so that they can be brought up into the chest when we're doing the chest component.
And lastly, botulinum toxin injection, which may or may not help.
I don't typically do a formal pylori myotomy on these patients.
I don't think you need it with a narrow conduit.
Whether or not Botox works, I think the evidence is a bit plus or minus.
But it's easy to do, and so we often just inject it.
The other way you can do this possibly better is endoscopically with an endoscope at the end of the case.
8mm port is going to be placed posteriorly, another one inferiorly, and then one up above the fourth or third rib.
It's helpful to have the patient rotated a little bit anteriorly to do this.
That allows the lung to sort of fall forward.
So the inferior pulmonary ligament is then taken down, staying right on the pericardium.
And you can see the area of the previous dissection from the abdominal portion of the procedure there.
And all that tissue at the hiatus is going to be resected on block with the esophageal specimen.

so the pleura on the anterior aspect of the esophagus as well as the posterior aspect is taken all the way up until we reach the bronchus at which point I typically will start using the bipolar dissecting device
really because there's less lateral spread of heat and it's a little bit more precise.
So in this particular patient, we are going to divide the esophagus in the region of the azagus vein, just below it actually.
It's a C-word type 2 tumor, so we're really situated at the gastroesophageal junction, and therefore no need to really go higher than the azagus.
So this is just dividing the pleura along the posterior aspect of the esophagus, and typically I would go to the level of the azagus vein.
I don't usually take the azagus vein, but we will take the thoracic duct in this situation, along with the specimen.
There's the duct there, and the aorta is deep to that.
So we're going to get around the thoracic duct fairly soon here and then divide that.
And you can tie it or you can use hemlock clips.
In this case, we're just going to clip it.
The subcarinal nodes are taken on block with the specimen.
And these can sometimes be a little bit challenging to take out without bleeding.
They're pretty well vascularized.
So I try to stay right on the pericardium as much as possible, sort of lift them up.
But one of the things that you often find is that the nodes fracture, and then they start bleeding, and it's a bit of a nuisance.
So there's the thoracic duct and the cephalad aspect of it.
I'll clip that.
Now the esophagus is being lifted up, sort of retracted laterally, and the remaining attachments to the aorta and pericardium are taken down.
Here I've switched the vessel sealing device from my right hand.
Now it's coming in through that posterior port just because the angle was better.
What you want to be careful of here is that you are not coming across the left main stem bronchus.
You see the left main stem bronchus there, the posterior membranous portion of it with the
left-sided endotracheal tube in it.
So the subcarotid nodes are just elevated off of the esophagus just to expose enough of the esophagus musculature that we can see where we're going to want to divide it.
So now this point we'll use endocyanin green again and near-infrared imaging and just to confirm that we are dividing the esophagus at a well-perfused portion.
and the entire esophagus there lights up, so anywhere there would be fine.
I usually use a cutting setting on the endoshears for this.
And so once the esophagus has been divided, then we will enlarge the anterior incision and take the specimen out through that anterior incision.
And as we pull the conduit up, then we'll just release it from the proximal stomach.
So if you don't attach the omentum to the stomach at this point, it's very, very difficult to get that up.
So we'll always suture the tip of the omentum to the proximal stomach.

Occasionally it is helpful here when you're pulling the conduit up to do this manually from an assist port because it's relatively easy to put too much tension on the conduit with the robot and then you can end up tearing the conduit.
So when I pull the rope up, I'm trying not to grasp the conduit, but rather just sort of passively pull it up.
And you can see it easily reaches the level of the azagus vein.
So now at this point we're going to do our purse string anastomosis and I'll use two 3-0 prolines.
The first one is a running horizontal mattress suture, making sure to get muscle as well as mucosa.
And we'll just run this all the way around.
And then the second one is going to be just a
Another 3-0, but this time just a running baseball, just to really tighten in all that excess tissue around the stem of the anvil.
I generally prefer to do this without the anvil in the lumen of the esophagus just because it gets in the way a little bit.
Often times we'll just sort of extrude and pop out when you don't want it to.
I would say in most cases we use an ILS or an EEA 25 millimeter circular stapler.
Occasionally, for people who've had more long-standing obstruction and they have a dilated esophagus, you can get a 29.
I would say most of the time it's a 25.
So once that's in, then the anvil of the stapler is passed by your assistant and then positioned in.
And the easiest way to grab these I found is with the needle driver.
Be very, very careful tying proline, particularly 3-0, because it will break.
And you have no tactile sensation about how much tension you're putting on the monofilament.
And so you really have to almost look to see the stretch in the suture.
And stop pulling just before it's about to snap.
But it can snap.
Just be careful of it.
So this is the second purse string.
We just sort of baseball that around just to sort of cinch in any excess tissue that might have gotten in the way of the stapler.
And then that's tied down.
So now at this point we're going to make a little gastronomy at the tip of the conduit.
Now this part's coming out anyhow so we don't really care about that.
And then we'll put our stapler in through that conduit and bring it out the back wall along the greater curvature.
It's always good to have a suction nearby because sometimes there's a spillage of gastric contents as soon as you open up that conduit.
The other thing we're going to do fairly soon here is we're going to move the camera from the camera position to the more cephalad port.
So now this is in the cephalad most port and the reason we do that is because this allows you to have a much better view of the stapler as it exits the back of the conduit.
It's a little awkward to get this ILS stapler in.
Once it's in, then we advance the spear.
By having the camera up high, we can see exactly where that spear is coming out of the back wall of the stomach.
That is a ProGrasp that I have in my right hand.
The reason I use a ProGrasp is that when we're trying to manipulate the anvil, a regular caddy air or a regular fenestrated bipolar really doesn't have the grip strength to be able to hold onto it.
So once that's docked, then we push additional tissue away from the underside of the anastomosis just to make sure that no fat or anything gets incorporated into the anastomosis.
Stapler's fired.
This is obviously being done by the assistant at the bedside.
Unfortunately there's no robotic circular stapler available.
We'll check our doughnuts at that point to make sure they're complete.
That little extra bit is just going to be divided.
taking care not to take that staple line too close to your circular staple line, otherwise there's going to be a relatively ischemic part of the stomach.
We're just going to over-sew the staple line then.
You probably don't need to do this, but certainly at the more ischemic end, I like to imbricate the staple line.
Then we move down to the hiatus, and I'll usually put about two or three little tacking sutures, and you can use a silk or a Vicrol, really just to secure the stomach to the edge of the diaphragm.
This is just in an effort to try and prevent herniation, although usually this does not occur on the right side, it's more often on the left side.
the left side is very difficult to secure.
Then we'll complete the over-sewing of the staple line.
And then the last thing we'll do is to position our mental flap so that it wraps itself around the conduit, protecting the conduit from the airway in the case of a leak.
And it's important when you wrap the flap that you secure the omentum to some structure such as the pleura or the mediastinal tissue just because otherwise it's going to just migrate.
So another chest tube is put in.
And then the cavity is irrigated, lung inflated.
